speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is April 5th, 1996. We are conducting an interview with survivor Joseph Gringlas. His name at birth was Joseph Grynglas. Interviewer is Kris Palmer. The city is Southfield, the state is Michigan, country is United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Kris Palmer. It's K-R-I-S P-A-L-M-E-R. Today's date is April 5th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Joseph Gringlas in Southfield, Michigan in the United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English. Joseph, I'd like to ask you first your name at birth, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Gringlas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your name spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-R-I-N-G-L-A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: At birth, did you have the same spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a different spelling-- instead of 'I' was a 'Y.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names that were given to you, nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and the country of your birth, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrowiec Swietokrzyski, and it's in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, could you spell the city for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-S-T-R-O-W-I-E-C and Swietokrzyski, S-W-capital K-C-Y-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Blima. Her maiden name was Fleishman. My father was Leizor [INAUDIBLE] Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had-- we were five brothers and one sister. The name of my oldest was Mendel, Shlomo, Jankel. My sister was Beila, and there was Sol. And I was the youngest, Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: All boys and one girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in my hometown in Poland, the same town what I told you before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this where your parents were from originally, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are originally from the same town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your parents meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did parents meet? [LAUGHS] I couldn't tell you that. They never-- never told me about it, and-- I don't know. They met-- this was not a big city. Probably-- probably through family, through acquaintance, or something. I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of industry went on in the town? Was it a farming community?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a farming-- farming and outside the city. But down in the city was a-- was a big factory. They built trains-- not passenger trains, commercial trains for loading things, you know, for shipment. And it had a [? hot ?] oven. There was like a-- they-- makes metal for-- metal for building trains. It built all kinds of metal. Anyway, in the same, there's a big oven. They use all kinds of equipment for the oven to bring the metal to liquid. It's a liquid. So that's what place I worked during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your father-- what did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did sell shoes. He used to go to big city, capital city of Warsaw. Brought it to my hometown, Ostrowiec. He used to have in the market, selling shoes. It was twice a week, Monday and Thursday, went to market for selling the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father like? What was he like as a person? What kinds of things was he interested in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was interested in be dressed up nice, holidays going to synagogue, and belonging to people, talking, and all kinds-- reading paper, newspapers, all kinds of stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family involved in the synagogue and involved in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were involved in praying, you know. We were members in a synagogue, you know. And I used to go every Saturday. My father-- I was the youngest one, so I used to have to go. So I went with my father to synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was an old synagogue. There was only one synagogue in that town. It was built a few-- I was told it was built a few hundred years ago. And it was destroyed after the Germans came in. They burned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was only-- only big-- there was other little, smaller ones, but this was the biggest in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who your rabbi was? Were you particularly close with your rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi, he-- he was known as Chaskiel. And that was a-- I remember about him, his father was very big. He was known over Poland. He was such a-- I mean, now, it's [INAUDIBLE] and everything that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- one thing I remember, my father, when he used to go to Warsaw to send special challahs-- you know the baked challahs for Shabbas? They sent them with him for the rabbi, which my father told me about it. But I-- at my-- at my time, I knew that was his son, because the father wasn't living anymore. His son, the rabbi was-- his name was Chaskiel. And to look at that rabbi was something like you look at a god, you know? He was dressed in a big hat, fur hat, and long, black things. It's different than you see here in this country, [LAUGHS] [? always. ?] He looked really different, you know-- very old-time. What was it like for you when you went to synagogue with your father? What did that feel like, and what was it like when you went in the door there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a happy time to be with my father. And I-- I was very close to my father, because I was the youngest son, so always close. Even when he went to Warsaw for shoes. In a time when-- when I was out of school, in vacation summertime, so we-- I went with him a few times to Warsaw and see how things going in business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mom-- what kinds of things did your mother do around the home? What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a housewife, really took care of the family, like, you know, if you have big family. So she was always busy cooking, cleaning, washing. And took care of us very, taking care of the way it should be taken care. And even when I-- you know, I used to go to cheder, you know. And she was very caring about. In summertime, you know, I couldn't go back to home. She would bring food to me and hide it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those things you never forget. I was a little boy. So she was very good mother. I had good parents, lovely parents. Did she have a lot of friends or family in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she had two sisters living in-- in the same town and had friends, you know. And, you know, they went to shul, synagogue, and they met family there all the time. So-- and we used to go to the other-- my aunt's family for the holidays and see the cousins and everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate the holidays like Passover? What kinds of things would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was different celebrated as here. It was-- like before Passover, they used to be putting in piece of bread in the evening. And before the day of Passover, used to take-- clean it up, bread, and put it together with a wooden spoon. And they would-- the next morning, they would burn it outside. It had to be burned. This was cleaning up from the bread, [? would ?] coming to starting eating matzahs. So that was, I remember, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I was closer to my brother which is a lot older from me, because we played soccer all the time with other friends. He was only four, four and a half years different from me. So I always played soccer in this-- with all other friends from the [? main ?] town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol. And he's the only survivor from my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were talking about playing soccer with your friends and things. What-- what other kinds of things did you do as a kid in your community growing up? What-- what was it like as a child there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember they used to come, friends, to my older brothers, and sister. We used to have parties when-- and they danced. And I was, at that time, about 9, 10 years old. And I-- I really paid attention to how they were doing the dance. So I would-- got into me, and I was dancing. I remember my sister used to go to a party, so I was about 10 years, 11 years old. And-- and I danced with her, like one of the youngest ones, because I love dancing. So it was-- it was always a party. It was always friends coming to the family. It was always happy feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a strong Jewish community in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very strong Jewish community. Very strong. You know, like I mentioned, went to cheder. And then after cheder, went to public school. So it was very strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any teachers, both religious teachers and then teachers in your public school, that you remember particularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember we had, as Jewish kids, used to have a religious teacher at religious hour. We went with our religious teacher, and-- and other kids went with the-- with the [? father, ?] the Christians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had separated that time. I'm remembering very good. I remember exactly what we learned about all history about Jewish history and-- and religion. Did you ever experience any anti-Semitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I experienced a lot of anti-Semitic experiences. When I went to cheder-- and at that time, when I went home, and at the time, the church was not far from the market where the cheder was. I was always hit in-- I was hit in the back. Somebody would run into me and was hitting me. I always came up crying. I was hit, and I didn't know why, what reason I was hit. But that was anti-Semitic. And other things-- I remember in the summertime, there was a-- a lake at our town. And we used to go swimming. And it was separate. They separated us. We couldn't swim with the Gentiles in same-- same river. So we had, like, a Jewish special place to go. And a lot of times, in there, they were throwing stones at us when we went through to get lake. So it was very bad experience, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents-- what did your parents say to you as a child? Did they ever try to explain this? Or was there anything they said to you that comforted you with this behavior?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible that we lived, and we have to take it. We can't do, because-- because there was-- nobody would help us to-- to clear this up and to do something for it. Because it's-- it's in that-- and the people [INAUDIBLE] in that anti-Semitic things that they-- what they couldn't do anything about it. So they'd be very, like, accustomed to it. It's supposed to be like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had friends-- nice, very nice people and weren't Jewish, yeah. Very, very close friends, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, by the way, I was-- where we lived was Gentiles. The landlords were the Gentiles. And I remember the one-- one daughter was my age and went to school. And her parents were anti-Semitic, but she was not. And she spoke Jewish like-- like us. She was very close. Shows you that-- usually, they think the parents anti-Semitic, the kids would be. But this was exceptional. She was very nice. Saturday, I didn't go to school, so Sunday day, she told me all the lessons what they had to do for next week. Very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hannah, ?] Ana, yeah. And her last name was Rogofska, R-O-G-O-F-S-K-I. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a lot of other friends, but she was in the same-- living the same apartment where we lived, so was very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your apartment like? What was your home like that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- [LAUGHS] it wasn't like kitchens and bedrooms. It was one big place, that's all. It was just stove [INAUDIBLE]. We washed ourselves-- it wasn't like a bathroom. We had a big, wooden things, I mean-- everyone had to go in and wash yourself. That was different. It's-- wasn't like here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that how most of the people in your town lived? Was that pretty typical of how the homes were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some, probably, other rich people, like lawyers or doctors, that had a little better-- better living. And I think they had better things in life. But the average was hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there professionals both in the Jewish and non-Jewish community? In other words, doctors, lawyers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, there were a lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jewish people allowed to have those professions in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There were doctors allowed, Jewish professionals, yeah. But for example, to go in a gymnasium, for us was-- we weren't able to do it. Only those people with a lot of-- rich people could do it, go to gymnasium, or study higher schooling. So the only thing-- public school was just-- was Jewish people. That shows you how different, how they treat us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of the town? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the population was-- I think it was 30,000 altogether. But it was 15,000, I think, Jewish people. And it looked like-- the town was actually, like, Jewish, because the stores were all Jewish. It looked like living in a Jewish-- but we were surrounded with Gentiles. And the church, as I told you before-- I had to go to the market. So-- and-- so but feel mostly we were around with Jewish people. But the landlord what where we lived were Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your street address where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I grew up-- it's called [NON-ENGLISH], which means 'market.' And it was number 51.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a boy, when did you sense that things were starting to change with the war effort? When did you start feeling like the war was coming close to home for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, before the war started, we had already newspapers and listened to radios that the Germany trying-- how they propagandas against the Jewish people. And how they-- they consider other people are lower class, and they're high-- higher morale, higher standard people. And then, before the war broke out, we knew that something was filling the air.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even I remember in school, geography, that one of the geography man-- the teacher said, 'See how the-- how the map is moving? It looks like the war's coming.' That's what I remember he said. That was just before the war broke out. What did your parents say about these reports? Did they talk with you about any of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Talk, but we couldn't do anything. But I didn't-- didn't think-- believe it's going to come to something like this. We thought, you know, we're going to be in trouble, but not something would happen. Nobody in right mind could think about, it's going to happen like that, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear reports on the radio as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel-- was there something in particular that maybe you experienced that made you feel like, boy, maybe the war's coming a little closer to my hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- we experienced it, because we-- it first was starting with Czechoslovakia. That was 1938. And the Germans tried to-- wanted to get a part of Czechoslovakia. At the time, I remember, I said-- I was very interested in reading paper. And it was the chamberlain, the prime minister from England, went to Berlin and made peace at that time before the war. [INAUDIBLE] And he came back to England, he said, 'We're going to have peace at our time.' But there was no peace. There was a peace on the paper. The war broke out a few months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13. I had just finished my public school. I was 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to really change in your town with the war coming close in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when the war started, and a few weeks later when the Germans came in. And I didn't know it was Germans, because England and France supposed to help Poland against the Germans. So a marching soldier-- so I went to other side of the street and-- where they're marching, to the soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the swastikas. And you [? heard ?] exactly that they're Germans. So that time, we started feeling that something really-- clouds is coming to us, something very bad things going to come. And then what happened? Right first day when they came in, they took out the doctors and lawyers, and they killed them out. The Jewish doctors and lawyers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we saw them laying on the ground. We heard it, and we saw it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Probably, because it's a small town. I mean, not small, but, I mean, it was 15,000. So everyone knew everyone else. And we heard about it. People would-- saw it, and they told us. And we knew that they weren't living anymore. They were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents talk about leaving at that time, or was there anything that could be done at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing could be done, because we were surrounded such a powerful army. We think it is no place to go for help, no way. I guess it was just the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your family, if we can start at the very beginning? Were you allowed to stay in your home for a period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were allowed to stay. But they wanted [INAUDIBLE] before they took us to work, they need some for cleaning street of snow. They went after the Jewish people, the young ones, to get us out in-- And at that times, they were hitting, and-- you know, and made you work, and screaming. And it felt terrible. That was the start of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father able to continue to work for a period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He were not allowed to any do-- go any selling, doing anything, no. He was not allowed to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your family eat and survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: So only thing-- they gave us rations, piece of bread, a certain bread of certain amount. I remember we had to stay in the back of the long lines to get the bread for the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This 19-- the war started in 1939. This was continuing in the '40s, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you forced to wear a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you forced to wear a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then there's-- they started-- it was not a yellow star. We had armband, a blue-- a white armband with blue stripes, and a star of David was on the side. And so that they knew exactly what-- who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you had to wear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I was about 14, 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to wear that everyday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there was warning everyday, find somebody, Jewish people or Jewish kids without their band-- they will be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did this mean to you when you had to wear the Jewish band?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew that they already-- they're, like, separating us from other people. Like trying to-- that-- for their-- you walk through, they know exactly what you are. And they would do anything what they want to do with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the youngest in your family, what did your brothers and sisters say? Did they talk with you about this? Were they worried about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were worried, but they couldn't do anything about it. You worry, but if you have something to do something about, you do it. But what are you going-- if there's nothing you can do help yourself, what are you going to do? But there was just-- we knew the problem. But as I told you before, nobody could imagine what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this start to change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: How'd it start changing my life? I mean, I felt very-- that we have no help, we're helpless. That makes you feel like you can-- you're very getting, like, paralyzed. You can't do free things what you want to do. You want to work, you want to go to a movie, you want to go out. You can't do that anymore. And you have to be like a ghetto, they start to make. You have to live all in the same place, same streets. So it-- it was terrible feeling. It was just helpless feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sent to a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was a ghetto in my hometown. There was a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you sent there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- what the-- where we lived, at the address I told you, the ghetto was there. It was close to the market, and a lot of Jewish people there. But people lived out of-- out of the ghetto had to move in into the ghetto. And people had to move with other families, because no place to stay, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the ghetto [? already. ?] They made this in there. They surrounded it with, like, signs, 'This is the ghetto,' like the Gentiles shouldn't go in there. Did you have other families at that time, then, move in with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, there's-- yeah, there was other people in. By that time already, Jewish people from close to the border of Germany that they already sent out, when the Jewish people-- when the Germans came in close to the German border, the Jewish people came to my hometown. And I remember exactly there was [INAUDIBLE] a place, Konin, K-O-N-I-N, and that's around [? Poznan ?] on the border of Germany. So they shipped them out from there they lived-- where they lived. So they came all, [? Konin, ?] [INAUDIBLE] towns. So I remember there was people living with my uncle and a lot of people in the same town, same ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto where you were detained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the ghetto was the name of my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time when you were in the ghetto, when your town was made into a ghetto-- your section of town-- what did you do day to day? How did you keep going? Were you given job assignments?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's-- the only thing what they did is they take that who needed for work, they went in the ghetto, and they-- they took us out to go to work for the Germans. And-- and the same time was, they made a Judenrat. Judenrat means some oldest people to organize for them what they wanted to do something for the people. Like they want people [INAUDIBLE] Judenrat, 'We need-- need 1,000 boys to work,' or something, so a few hundred boys [INAUDIBLE]. So they took us. They had the order from them to do it. Or they-- they want money. They should get money out of the Jewish people. And otherwise, they're going to kill us out, or something like that, they're warning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what the Judenrat was. They did what they want, tell them what to do. And then you had to do all the-- do things for them. Did you know people who were in the Judenrat in your ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I mean, we know the people, yeah. We know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there animosity between the people living in the ghetto and the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, but you couldn't do anything. We knew that they-- they have to do what they wanted-- told them to do. And-- and they thought they were going to do it like going to work, giving what-- the money what they want, that's [INAUDIBLE] quiet them down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did it. Then, you know, they did-- they what they told them to do. And they-- the Nazi told them, they did it. What kind of work were you forced to do during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked-- OK, cleaning, like snow cleaning, or something in the kitchens for the Germans for helping out. And all kinds, you know, manual work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers and your sister working also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there's only-- with me was the older brother from me. And-- but the other brothers were married, and they lived separate, anyway. So they wasn't working. They had to do something, helping out with themselves. But they wasn't working with us two together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they living in the same ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They living in town, yeah. But not with us, the ghetto, because there were different people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you forced during this time to work in a factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, yeah. They took us to work the factory, you know, the factory I used to work, they call it putting trains-- the train, where they go-- they do, those metal sides for the train. And we work on those train. They'd start [INAUDIBLE] with that. You know, wood, whatever was bad things, we have to-- for fix it up. That's when they started working [? with ?] that. That's where they got me in the-- the factory working. And we know if anybody's going to work for awhile, then meanwhile, they're going to need us. Then we'll-- we'll-- we won't survive, but for-- for a while, being interconnected, they need us, so they're not going to kill us out. So that's what then happened when they start taking out all the Jewish people. And I'll tell-- tell you what I told you about before.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went to work in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- let's see, that was in 1942. I was about 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were your brothers working in the factory with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, there's only brother that older from me, Sol, worked with me. And I told you about that special oven, they make metal. And that's-- he was loading those, all kinds of material, for the oven. And I was unloading big trains. It's called cox, C-O-X. It's something-- a material. It's light but gives a lot of heat for the metal. And I was unloading the trains. And the trains was bigger than this house. Unloading them, about two or three boys and a wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your other brothers, during this time, were they working at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They worked, yeah. They worked with civilians, the other people. Did some work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's only one. The oldest one had a child, but-- but what happened during the-- they took us out in market. Maybe I told you about this. It was a Sunday. And Saturday night, I worked at night shift in the factory. So before I went to the factory, I looked at my parents, and thought, this is the last time I'm seeing them. And my brother was working during daylight, so he was in the time when they took us-- took out the Jewish people all from all in the market to send them away. So he was on the list from working with me in the-- the factory. So he came down in the evening. He told me that the oldest son was-- had a baby. It was a bris, where the Jewish people-- they come to circumcise. I didn't see him, but he saw it. But that's-- then they had to go out. And they made everybody get-- had to get out.
